name: French Defense
description: A solid, strategic defense where Black builds a pawn chain and fights for counterplay against White's center. Known for its closed, positional character.

moves.commentary: Take the center.
The French Defense — they're preparing d5. It's solid but locks in their light-squared bishop. That's their long-term problem.
Two pawns in the center. You're dominating space right now.
They challenge your e4 pawn. This tension — d5 vs e4 — is the heart of every French Defense.
Defend e4 with the knight. Now they choose their weapon...
The Winawer! They pin your knight and put maximum pressure on e4. This is the most dynamic and aggressive French variation.
Push forward! You gain space and close the center. Now it's a pawn chain battle.
They attack the base of your chain at d4. Classic French strategy — chip away at the foundation.
Force the question: what's that bishop doing? They have to commit.
They trade bishop for knight. You get doubled pawns but keep the bishop pair — it's a fair trade.
Your pawn structure is ugly but your bishops are powerful. That's the deal in the Winawer.
They develop behind the pawn chain. That knight is heading to f5 eventually.
Queen goes aggressive! You're targeting g7 — they have dark-square weaknesses after giving up their bishop.
They defend g7 and eye your e5 pawn. A sharp battle ahead — your bishops vs their structure.
Center.
French Defense.
Full center.
They challenge e4.
Defend e4.
The Classical French — they develop the knight and keep pressing e4. More traditional than the Winawer.
Pin that knight! Now they face a dilemma: if they play Be7 and you trade, their kingside pawns get messed up.
They choose stability. They'll deal with the pin later.
Push! Gain space and drive their knight back. Your pawn chain is imposing.
Knight retreats but stays flexible — it can go to c5 or support f6 later.
Trade the bishop. You're removing a defender of the dark squares around their king.
They recapture with the queen. Still a solid position for them.
Reinforce e5 and grab more kingside space. You're building a slow kingside attack.
They castle. They'll try to break with c5 on the queenside — that's their plan.
Develop your last knight. You've got space and a clear plan: push on the kingside. They'll push on the queenside. Let the battle begin.
Center.
French.
Full center.
They challenge.
The Tarrasch! You defend e4 with the knight on d2 instead of c3. It avoids the Winawer pin but the knight is a bit passive here.
They develop and pressure e4.
Push forward, grab space. Same idea as other French lines.
Knight retreats, ready to attack your chain with c5.
Great diagonal for this bishop — it's pointing at the kingside. Your attacking piece.
Here comes their break! They're going after d4, the base of your chain.
Prop up d4. You need that pawn chain to hold.
More pressure on d4. They're ganging up on it.
Develop without blocking the bishop. This knight can go to f4 or g3 — flexible.
They break through on d4.
Take back. Your center holds. A classic French middlegame — you attack kingside, they counterattack in the center and queenside.
Center.
French.
Two pawns in the center.
They challenge e4.
The Advance Variation — you push immediately. Simple idea: grab space. But your pawn structure is now fixed, so they know exactly where to attack.
Right on cue — they go after d4. This is THE move in the Advance French.
Hold d4 with the c-pawn. Your chain needs to be solid.
More pressure on d4. They're not letting up.
Develop and support d4. Piece by piece, you build.
Queen comes out hitting b2 and d4. Two targets at once — that's annoying.
Develop and prepare to castle. Don't panic about b2 — you'll deal with it.
Looks weird, right? But that knight is heading to f5 where it'll pressure d4 and e3. Clever.
Trade it off before it reaches f5. Sometimes preventing their plan is worth giving up the bishop.
They get doubled pawns but an open g-file. If they castle queenside, that g-file could be dangerous. Sharp position for both sides.
